name: Zimbabwe
tagline: From the great medieval stone city of the Shona, through the Mutapa and Rozvi states, the Ndebele kingdom, British colonisation as Southern Rhodesia, and a long liberation war to independence in 1980.
summary: Zimbabwe takes its name from Great Zimbabwe, the largest medieval stone city in sub-Saharan Africa, built by ancestors of the Shona between roughly the 11th and 15th centuries and grown rich on a gold trade that reached the Swahili coast and the Indian Ocean world. Great Zimbabwe's decline was followed by the Mutapa empire and the Rozvi and Torwa states, whose dry-stone tradition survives at Khami. In the nineteenth century the Ndebele kingdom of Mzilikazi and his son Lobengula dominated the south-west, until Cecil Rhodes's British South Africa Company secured the Rudd Concession in 1888, sent the Pioneer Column north in 1890, and seized the country, which became the self-governing colony of Southern Rhodesia. Early African resistance in the risings of 1896–97 — remembered as the First Chimurenga — was crushed, opening decades of white-minority rule. In 1965 Ian Smith's government issued a Unilateral Declaration of Independence; the liberation war, the Second Chimurenga, was waged by ZANU under Robert Mugabe and ZAPU under Joshua Nkomo until the Lancaster House Agreement of December 1979 paved the way to internationally recognised independence on 18 April 1980. The decades since have been marked by the Gukurahundi massacres in Matabeleland in the 1980s, the chaotic fast-track land reform and economic collapse of the 2000s, and the removal of Mugabe in 2017.

founding.label: Independence from the United Kingdom
founding.detail: Zimbabwe became an internationally recognised independent state at midnight on 18 April 1980, ending nearly a century of colonial and white-minority rule that had begun with the British South Africa Company's occupation in 1890. Independence followed the Lancaster House Agreement of 21 December 1979, which ended the liberation war (the Second Chimurenga) and provided for a ceasefire, a new constitution and supervised elections. Robert Mugabe's ZANU(PF) won the February 1980 election, and Mugabe became the country's first prime minister. The new nation took its name from Great Zimbabwe, the medieval Shona stone city whose ruins had stood as a symbol of African achievement throughout the colonial era.

quickFacts.label: Independence
quickFacts.value: From the United Kingdom on 18 April 1980, after the Lancaster House Agreement of December 1979

quickFacts.label: Namesake
quickFacts.value: Great Zimbabwe, a medieval Shona stone city; the name means roughly 'houses of stone' (dzimba dza mabwe)

quickFacts.label: First leader
quickFacts.value: Robert Mugabe led ZANU(PF) to victory in 1980 and ruled until forced from office in November 2017

quickFacts.label: Capital
quickFacts.value: Harare, founded in 1890 as Fort Salisbury by the Pioneer Column and renamed in 1982

quickFacts.label: UNESCO sites
quickFacts.value: Great Zimbabwe (1986), Khami Ruins (1986), Mana Pools, Victoria Falls and the Matobo Hills

quickFacts.label: Economy
quickFacts.value: Hyperinflation peaked in 2008, leading the country to abandon its own currency in 2009

eras.title: Great Zimbabwe and the gold trade
eras.standfirst: Ancestors of the Shona raised the largest stone city in sub-Saharan Africa, controlling a gold and ivory trade that reached the Swahili coast and beyond.
body: From around the eleventh century, on the granite uplands of the south-eastern plateau, ancestors of the Shona-speaking peoples built Great Zimbabwe, a city whose dry-stone walls — laid without mortar — remain the largest ancient stone structures in sub-Saharan Africa. The Shona word for such places, dzimba dza mabwe, 'houses of stone', gave the modern nation its name. At its height in the thirteenth and fourteenth centuries the city and its surrounding settlement may have held on the order of ten thousand to perhaps eighteen thousand people, ruled by an elite who occupied the hilltop and valley enclosures.

Great Zimbabwe's wealth rested on cattle and, above all, on long-distance trade. Gold mined and panned across the plateau, together with ivory, was carried eastward to the Swahili-coast ports of Sofala and Kilwa and exchanged for goods from across the Indian Ocean. Archaeologists have recovered Chinese porcelain, Persian and Near Eastern glass beads, and a coin from Kilwa among the ruins, evidence of a community plugged into a trading world that stretched to Arabia, India and China. The Great Enclosure, with walls reaching some eleven metres in height, and the solid Conical Tower within it are the most celebrated of the surviving structures.

By the fifteenth century the city was being abandoned, for reasons still debated: a likely combination of soil exhaustion, the overgrazing and deforestation of an over-large population, declining local gold, and the northward shift of trade routes toward the Zambezi. The dispersal of its people did not end the civilisation but transferred its centre of gravity, as new Shona states rose to inherit Great Zimbabwe's role in the gold trade. The ruins, encountered by Europeans in the nineteenth century, were long misattributed by colonial writers to foreign builders before archaeology firmly established their African origin; the site was inscribed by UNESCO as a World Heritage Site in 1986.

events.title: Great Zimbabwe is built
events.summary: Ancestors of the Shona began raising the mortarless granite walls of Great Zimbabwe, which would become the largest stone city in sub-Saharan Africa and give the modern nation its name.

events.title: Apex of the gold trade
events.summary: At its height the city held perhaps 10,000–18,000 people and controlled a gold and ivory trade reaching the Swahili coast at Sofala and Kilwa; Chinese porcelain, Persian glass and a Kilwa coin have been excavated there.

events.title: Decline and abandonment
events.summary: Great Zimbabwe was abandoned, probably owing to resource exhaustion and shifting trade routes, as the centre of power moved north toward the Zambezi and successor states.

events.title: Inscribed as a World Heritage Site
events.summary: UNESCO inscribed Great Zimbabwe National Monument as a World Heritage Site, recognising it as a unique testimony to the Bantu civilisation of the Shona between the 11th and 15th centuries.

pullquote.text: The Shona named such places dzimba dza mabwe — houses of stone — and from those words the modern nation took its name.

eras.title: Mutapa, Torwa and Rozvi
eras.standfirst: Successor states inherited Great Zimbabwe's gold trade — the Mutapa empire toward the Zambezi, and the Torwa and Rozvi states centred on Khami in the south-west.
body: As Great Zimbabwe waned, its political tradition passed to successor states. To the north, toward the middle Zambezi, the Mutapa empire (Mwenemutapa, rendered by the Portuguese as Monomotapa) rose in the fifteenth century under rulers who carried the title Mwenemutapa, 'lord of the conquered lands'. Mutapa controlled much of the northern plateau and parts of present-day Mozambique and continued the export of gold and ivory toward the coast.

From about 1505 the Portuguese, established at Sofala and on the Zambezi, sought to control this trade and intervened increasingly in Mutapa's affairs, planting trading fairs (feiras) in the interior and at times installing client rulers. Their pressure weakened the empire over the following century. In the south-west, meanwhile, the Torwa state arose with its capital at Khami, near present-day Bulawayo, carrying forward the dry-stone building tradition in elaborately decorated walled terraces; Khami too is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

In the later seventeenth century a new Shona power, the Rozvi (Changamire) state, emerged under the warrior-ruler Changamire Dombo. The Rozvi absorbed the Torwa, drove the Portuguese trading fairs from the plateau around the 1690s, and dominated much of the region into the early nineteenth century. Their hegemony was shattered in the 1830s by the upheavals of the Mfecane, as armed migrant groups — including the Ndebele moving up from the south — swept across the plateau and ended Rozvi power.

events.title: Rise of the Mutapa empire
events.summary: The Mutapa (Mwenemutapa) empire emerged toward the Zambezi, inheriting Great Zimbabwe's gold trade and ruling much of the northern plateau and parts of present-day Mozambique.

events.title: Portuguese intervention in the gold trade
events.summary: Portuguese traders based at Sofala and on the Zambezi pressed into the interior, establishing fairs and interfering in Mutapa politics, steadily weakening the empire over the next century.

events.title: The Torwa state and Khami
events.summary: In the south-west the Torwa state built its capital at Khami near present-day Bulawayo, continuing the stone-walling tradition in decorated terraces; Khami is now a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

events.title: The Rozvi state under Changamire Dombo
events.summary: Changamire Dombo founded the Rozvi state, absorbing the Torwa and expelling Portuguese fairs from the plateau around the 1690s; Rozvi power dominated the region until the Mfecane in the 1830s.

figures.name: Changamire Dombo
figures.blurb: Shona warrior-ruler who built the Rozvi (Changamire) state, absorbed the Torwa kingdom and drove Portuguese traders from the Zimbabwe plateau around the 1690s.

eras.title: The Ndebele kingdom and British conquest
eras.standfirst: Mzilikazi's Ndebele built a kingdom in the south-west; then the Rudd Concession, the Pioneer Column and two wars delivered the country to Cecil Rhodes's British South Africa Company as Southern Rhodesia.
body: Driven north during the Mfecane, the upheavals unleashed by the rise of the Zulu kingdom, the Ndebele (Matabele) under Mzilikazi settled in the south-western plateau around 1840, founding a centralised military kingdom centred on Bulawayo and overlaying the Shona societies of the region. On Mzilikazi's death in 1868 his son Lobengula became king after a contested succession, ruling a powerful state that nonetheless faced mounting European pressure for its land and rumoured gold.

In 1888 Lobengula was induced to sign the Rudd Concession, granting Cecil Rhodes's associates exclusive mining rights; Rhodes used it to obtain a royal charter for his British South Africa Company in 1889. In 1890 the Company despatched the Pioneer Column, a column of settlers and police, into Mashonaland, where on 13 September it raised the flag at the site of Fort Salisbury — the future Harare. The Ndebele kingdom was destroyed in the First Matabele War of 1893; Lobengula fled and died, and the Company seized Matabeleland.

In 1896–97 the Ndebele and then the Shona rose against Company rule in revolts later honoured as the First Chimurenga, in which spirit mediums such as Nehanda and Kaguvi played a leading part. The risings were crushed and their leaders executed, entrenching settler control. The territory, named Rhodesia after Rhodes, was governed by the Company until 1923, when its white settlers voted for self-government and it became the self-governing British colony of Southern Rhodesia, with land and political power reserved overwhelmingly for the white minority.

events.title: The Ndebele settle the south-west
events.summary: Mzilikazi led the Ndebele north during the Mfecane and established a centralised military kingdom around Bulawayo, dominating the south-western plateau and its Shona inhabitants.

events.title: Rudd Concession and the Pioneer Column
events.summary: Lobengula signed the 1888 Rudd Concession granting mining rights to Rhodes's agents; Rhodes won a royal charter in 1889 and in 1890 the British South Africa Company's Pioneer Column occupied Mashonaland, raising the flag at Fort Salisbury on 13 September.

events.title: First Matabele War
events.summary: Company forces invaded and destroyed the Ndebele kingdom; Lobengula fled and soon died, and the British South Africa Company annexed Matabeleland.

events.title: First Chimurenga risings
events.summary: The Ndebele and Shona rose against Company rule in revolts inspired in part by spirit mediums such as Nehanda and Kaguvi; the risings were crushed and their leaders executed.

events.title: Southern Rhodesia becomes a self-governing colony
events.summary: After a settler referendum, Company rule ended and Southern Rhodesia became a self-governing British colony, with land and political power reserved for the white minority.

figures.name: Lobengula
figures.blurb: Son of Mzilikazi who ruled the Ndebele kingdom from 1868, signed the 1888 Rudd Concession under pressure, and saw his state destroyed by the British South Africa Company in 1893 before his death.

figures.name: Cecil Rhodes
figures.role: Founder of the British South Africa Company
figures.blurb: British mining magnate and imperialist whose chartered company conquered the country in 1890 and named it Rhodesia, driving the colonisation of present-day Zimbabwe and Zambia.

pullquote.text: The risings of 1896–97 — the First Chimurenga — were crushed, but their memory would inspire the liberation war eight decades later.

eras.title: White rule, UDI and the liberation war
eras.standfirst: Decades of white-minority rule led to Ian Smith's Unilateral Declaration of Independence in 1965 and a long liberation war — the Second Chimurenga — that ended in majority rule.
body: Under settler self-government, laws such as the 1930 Land Apportionment Act entrenched racial division, reserving the best land for whites and confining most Africans to smaller, poorer areas. From 1953 to 1963 Southern Rhodesia was joined with Northern Rhodesia and Nyasaland in the Federation of Rhodesia and Nyasaland, but African nationalism grew. In 1963 rival movements emerged — the Zimbabwe African People's Union (ZAPU) under Joshua Nkomo, drawing strongly on Ndebele support, and the breakaway Zimbabwe African National Union (ZANU), associated with Shona leaders including Robert Mugabe.

Determined to preserve white rule, Prime Minister Ian Smith and his Rhodesian Front issued a Unilateral Declaration of Independence from Britain on 11 November 1965. Britain refused to recognise it and, with the United Nations, imposed sanctions, but did not intervene by force. The illegal regime held out for a decade and a half. From the mid-1960s the nationalist movements launched a guerrilla war — the Second Chimurenga or Rhodesian Bush War — with ZANU's ZANLA operating largely from Mozambique and ZAPU's ZIPRA from Zambia.

By the late 1970s the war, sanctions and regional pressure made the position untenable. An internal settlement produced the short-lived 'Zimbabwe Rhodesia' under Bishop Abel Muzorewa in 1979, but it won no international recognition. Britain convened all parties at Lancaster House in London, and on 21 December 1979 the Lancaster House Agreement provided for a ceasefire, a new constitution and supervised elections. Mugabe's ZANU(PF) won the February 1980 poll, and at midnight on 18 April 1980 Zimbabwe became an independent, internationally recognised state.

events.title: Land Apportionment Act
events.summary: Settler legislation formalised racial division of land, reserving the most productive areas for the white minority and restricting African landholding, a grievance that fuelled later nationalism.

events.title: Founding of ZANU and ZAPU
events.summary: African nationalism crystallised in two movements: ZAPU under Joshua Nkomo and the breakaway ZANU, soon led toward armed struggle by figures including Robert Mugabe.

events.title: Unilateral Declaration of Independence
events.summary: Ian Smith's Rhodesian Front declared independence from Britain to preserve white-minority rule; Britain and the UN refused recognition and imposed sanctions.

events.title: The liberation war (Second Chimurenga)
events.summary: Guerrilla forces of ZANU (ZANLA) and ZAPU (ZIPRA), based in Mozambique and Zambia, waged an escalating war against the Rhodesian state through the 1970s.

events.title: Lancaster House Agreement
events.summary: Talks in London produced an agreement providing for a ceasefire, a new constitution and British-supervised elections, ending the war and opening the path to majority rule.

figures.name: Ian Smith
figures.blurb: Leader of the Rhodesian Front who issued the 1965 Unilateral Declaration of Independence and led the white-minority regime through the liberation war until the Lancaster House settlement.

figures.name: Joshua Nkomo
figures.blurb: Veteran nationalist who founded and led ZAPU, a central figure in the liberation struggle and, after the 1987 Unity Accord, a vice-president of Zimbabwe.

pullquote.text: At midnight on 18 April 1980, after fifteen years of UDI and a long war, the flag of independent Zimbabwe was raised.

eras.title: Independence, Gukurahundi and the Mugabe years
eras.standfirst: Independence under Mugabe was scarred by the Gukurahundi massacres of the 1980s, the land reform and hyperinflation of the 2000s, and Mugabe's removal in 2017.
body: Robert Mugabe became prime minister at independence on 18 April 1980 and dominated Zimbabwean politics for the next thirty-seven years. The early hopes of reconciliation were soon shadowed by violence. Between 1983 and 1987, in a campaign known as Gukurahundi, the army's North Korean-trained Fifth Brigade carried out massacres in Matabeleland and the Midlands, targeting civilians and supporters of Nkomo's ZAPU. Estimates of the dead range widely, from around 3,000 to as many as 20,000 or more; the violence ended only with the Unity Accord of 22 December 1987, which merged ZAPU into ZANU(PF) and made Mugabe executive president.

After 2000 the government launched a 'fast-track' land reform, encouraging often-violent seizures of white-owned commercial farms by war veterans and others. Combined with mismanagement, drought and sanctions, the disruption helped collapse the economy. Inflation spiralled into one of the worst hyperinflations in recorded history, peaking around mid-November 2008, after which Zimbabwe abandoned its own currency in 2009 in favour of foreign currencies such as the US dollar. Disputed elections, the contested 2008 poll and a subsequent power-sharing government with the opposition leader Morgan Tsvangirai marked a period of deep political and economic crisis.

In November 2017 the army intervened after Mugabe dismissed his vice-president Emmerson Mnangagwa; under military pressure and facing impeachment, Mugabe resigned on 21 November 2017, ending his rule, and Mnangagwa became president. Mugabe died in 2019. Zimbabwe has since continued to struggle with economic instability, contested elections and recurring currency crises, even as Great Zimbabwe and the country's other heritage sites remain enduring symbols of a deep and distinctively African past.

events.title: Independence under Robert Mugabe
events.summary: Zimbabwe became an internationally recognised independent state, with Mugabe's ZANU(PF) in power after winning the February 1980 election.

events.title: The Gukurahundi massacres
events.summary: The army's Fifth Brigade carried out massacres in Matabeleland and the Midlands targeting ZAPU supporters and Ndebele civilians; estimates of the dead range from several thousand to over twenty thousand. The campaign ended with the 1987 Unity Accord.

events.title: Fast-track land reform
events.summary: The government backed often-violent seizures of white-owned commercial farms; the disruption, with drought and sanctions, helped trigger a severe economic collapse.

events.title: Hyperinflation and dollarisation
events.summary: Inflation reached among the highest rates ever recorded, peaking in late 2008; in 2009 Zimbabwe abandoned its own currency for foreign ones such as the US dollar.

events.title: Mugabe forced from power
events.summary: After a military intervention and the threat of impeachment, Robert Mugabe resigned after thirty-seven years in power, and Emmerson Mnangagwa became president.

figures.name: Robert Mugabe
figures.role: Prime Minister and President of Zimbabwe
figures.blurb: Liberation-war leader of ZANU who became Zimbabwe's first prime minister in 1980 and later executive president, ruling for thirty-seven years until forced from office in 2017; his rule encompassed both independence and decades of repression and economic decline.

pullquote.text: The state's own commission of inquiry into the Gukurahundi was never published; the toll has been estimated in the thousands to tens of thousands.

figures.name: Mzilikazi
figures.blurb: Zulu general who, during the Mfecane, led his followers north to found the Ndebele (Matabele) kingdom in the south-west of present-day Zimbabwe, with its capital near Bulawayo.

figures.name: Lobengula
figures.blurb: Mzilikazi's son and successor, whose signing of the 1888 Rudd Concession opened the way to Cecil Rhodes's conquest; his kingdom was destroyed in the 1893 war.

figures.name: Robert Mugabe
figures.blurb: Led ZANU through the liberation war and governed Zimbabwe from independence in 1980 until he was forced from office in 2017.
